label: lassak_mccarthy::tea_tree_melaleuca
text: Melaleuca alternifolia — Tea Tree. Published ethnobotanical record with documented Bundjalung community involvement. From Lassak & McCarthy, Australian Medicinal Plants (1983) and Brophy et al., CSIRO Plant Industry. Bundjalung people, northern New South Wales: Leaves crushed and used as inhalant for coughs, colds, and headaches. Crushed leaves applied as poultice to wounds, cuts, burns, and skin infections. Bathing in streams and pools where Melaleuca grew — recognised as healing waters. Traditional preparation: fresh leaves crushed between hands and inhaled directly; or leaves wrapped around wound. Note: This documentation involves published academic literature with noted community collaboration. The Bundjalung knowledge of tea tree predates European contact by tens of thousands of years. Active compounds: Terpinen-4-ol (major antimicrobial component, >30% by standard), gamma-terpinene, alpha-terpinene, 1,8-cineole. Mechanism: terpinen-4-ol disrupts bacterial cell membrane integrity; active against Staphylococcus aureus (including MRSA), Candida albicans, Propionibacterium acnes. European Pharmacopoeia standard established. Multiple clinical trials: wound healing, acne (comparable to benzoyl peroxide in RCT), oral health.

label: lassak_mccarthy::eucalyptus_medicinal
text: Eucalyptus species (multiple) — Gum Trees. Published records from multiple Australian Aboriginal groups (Lassak & McCarthy 1983; Clarke 2008, Aboriginal Plant Use in South-Eastern Australia). Aboriginal uses documented with community involvement: Leaves heated and vapour inhaled for respiratory conditions (cough, cold, congestion, fever). Warm leaf infusion as chest rub. Bark used for wound wrapping. Resin (kino) for sore throats and as antiseptic. The diversity of eucalyptus-using practices across geographically separated Aboriginal groups — despite absence of documented contact — represents internal Australian convergence. Over 700 eucalyptus species in Australia; Aboriginal communities developed pharmacological knowledge of the species in their specific territory. Active compounds: 1,8-Cineole (eucalyptol) — major compound in most species; expectorant, bronchodilator, antimicrobial. Multiple clinical trials: eucalyptus oil for COPD (positive), sinusitis (positive), dental antimicrobial. Eucalyptus global spread: now cultivated on every inhabited continent — one of the most successful plant transplants in history, with medicinal use following its spread. ⚠️ Content from published literature with documented community involvement only.

label: lassak_mccarthy::kakadu_plum_terminalia
text: Kakadu Plum (Terminalia ferdinandiana) — Gubinge / Murunga. Published scientific literature (Netzel et al. 2007, Journal of Food Composition and Analysis; Gong et al. 2018, multiple papers). Aboriginal groups of the Northern Territory and Western Australia: Small sour fruit eaten fresh and dried. Used for its fortifying and disease-preventing properties — described as 'medicine food' rather than separating food and medicine functions (consistent with traditional pharmacopeias globally). Modern nutritional analysis: vitamin C content 2,000–5,300mg per 100g fresh fruit — highest documented vitamin C content of any food on Earth, approximately 100x the vitamin C of oranges. Also contains ellagic acid, gallic acid — same compounds as in the Tibetan/Ayurvedic Terminalia chebula (arura). Cross-tradition convergence: Terminalia species are the most medicinally important genus in Tibetan medicine (Three Fruits formula), in Ayurveda (Triphala), and in Australian Indigenous medicine — three completely independent traditions independently identifying Terminalia species as having exceptional medicinal/nutritional properties. Now commercially available; growing market. ⚠️ Published literature only — no sacred or restricted knowledge.

label: lassak_mccarthy::emu_bush_eremophila
text: Emu Bush (Eremophila longifolia / E. alternifolia) — Weena (Arrernte) / Berrigan (widespread name). Published: Palombo & Semple (2001), Journal of Applied Microbiology; Cock & Mohanty (2011). Aboriginal groups of central and southern Australia: Leaves boiled in water, solution used to bathe wounds, sores, and skin infections. Steam inhaled for respiratory conditions. Hot leaf poultice applied to aching joints. Leaves waved in fire smoke and used for cleansing. Modern analysis (Palombo & Semple 2001): Eremophila longifolia leaf extract demonstrated strong antimicrobial activity against Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus, and Helicobacter pylori. Subsequent studies: activity against MRSA confirmed. Active compounds identified: serrulatane diterpenes (unique chemical class not found in plants from any other continent) — completely novel antimicrobial compounds. Research status: pre-clinical; no clinical trials yet. Potential significance: novel chemical class with no known antibiotic resistance, representing exactly the kind of untapped pharmacological territory the project aims to identify. ⚠️ Published literature only.
